office_does: A state representative serves a two-year term in Washington's 98-member House, voting on state budgets, taxes, and laws covering schools, housing, healthcare, transportation, climate, and civil rights.
race_blurb: An open seat after longtime Rep. Larry Springer's retirement, with two Democrats — Redmond City Council President Vanessa Kritzer and technology attorney Chandler Torbett — treated as the serious contenders; Republican John P Gibbons filed no statement or fundraising.

candidates.summary: Redmond City Councilmember (2020–present) and Council President, and Executive Director of the Washington Association of Land Trusts, running on her municipal housing, tenant-protection, and climate record.
highlights: Six-plus years on the Redmond City Council, including as Council President; strengthened inclusionary zoning and led creation of a renter-protections code chapter [S4]
Supports progressive revenue including recent high-earner taxes and is open to broader structural reform such as an income tax [S4]
Record includes Redmond's climate emergency declaration, environmental action plan, and salmon recovery leadership [S4]; leads with climate, housing, and childcare [S1]
Holds the 45th District Democrats, WEA, State Labor Council, and most area electeds [S1]; leads fundraising with about $109K reported [S3]
Backed a Redmond resolution funding organizations serving immigrants and refugees; wants state anti-discrimination and healthcare-access backstops [S4]

taxes.basis: Calls the tax code deeply regressive, supports high-earner taxes, and is open to structural income-tax reform and head-tax-style tools.

spending.basis: Platform centers public investment in housing, childcare, and climate; Seattle Times criticized her for naming no disagreement with her party's spending direction.

housing.basis: Led renter-protections code chapter, supports rent stabilization and permanent supportive housing, though paired with supply and zoning work.

climate.basis: Career conservation leadership plus a council record of climate emergency declaration, environmental action plan, and Climate Commitment Act funding.

experience.basis: The elected-record candidate in the race: six-plus years on the Redmond council with enacted housing and climate policy.

local-control.basis: Supports a stronger state role setting 'the floor' on housing production and calls affordability a regional/statewide problem.

immigration.basis: Backed a Redmond resolution affirming protections and funding for organizations serving immigrants and refugees.

social.basis: Pledges to defend civil rights with state anti-discrimination and healthcare-access backstops; endorsed by Planned Parenthood Alliance.

candidates.summary: A technology attorney and first-time candidate raised on the Muscogee Nation reservation, running on affordability, universal pre-K, AI consumer protections, and government accountability; endorsed by the Seattle Times.
highlights: Centers affordability — housing, childcare, groceries, insurance — and pledges urgency and execution over ideology [S4]
Supports the million-dollar earners income tax and capital gains tax but says new taxes should come with broad-based relief [S4]
The only candidate centering AI regulation: wants consumer-protection guardrails on AI in hiring, healthcare, and finance while encouraging innovation [S4]
Says the Legislature must 'prove we can use this money wisely' and is open to market-based solutions in some cases [S5]
Endorsed by the Seattle Times editorial board and by retiring Rep. Larry Springer (who endorsed both Democrats) [S5]

taxes.basis: Supports progressive revenue including the high-earner income tax and capital gains tax, but criticizes tax packages that lack broad-based relief.

spending.basis: Runs on accountability and delivery — 'We've got to prove we can use this money wisely' — while still backing new programs like universal pre-K.

housing.basis: Emphasizes supply through zoning reform and density near transit; no tenant-protection or public-housing platform in the dossier.

climate.basis: Wants to accelerate the clean-energy transition, primarily by removing permitting and administrative obstacles.

experience.basis: First-time candidate with no elected record, running on fresh execution and lived experience against a six-year councilmember.

tech.basis: Wants AI consumer-protection guardrails in hiring, healthcare, and financial decisions, balanced with encouraging innovation and jobs.

candidates.summary: The sole Republican-preference candidate in the race; he submitted no pamphlet statement, lists no website, and shows no reported fundraising.
highlights: Provided no statement, platform, biography, or website to the voters' pamphlet [S1]
No contributions for a 2026 campaign appear in PDC data [S2]
Likely the same John Gibbons who lost LD45 House general elections in 2020 and 2022 by roughly 45-point margins, though identity could not be conclusively verified [S3]
